# Marina Jovic (Academic Writing)

<!-- vimeo: 1111472466 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

<!-- vid-ref:1111472466 -->
[▶ Watch on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1111472466)


I could not and did not want to ban AI in my classroom. Instead, I wanted to see what would happen if students used it thoughtfully, critically, and with purpose. My name is Dr. Marina Jovi and I work for Golf University for Science and Technology in Kuwait. I teach academic writing and linguistic courses. And today's topic that I will be addressing is AI and academic writing strategies for maintaining cognitive develop development. I worked on a number of publications and I took some roles related to ai, which included developing AI policy on an institutional level and serving as a conference committee chair for AI innovations. In translation, education, literature and linguistics, generative AI tools have rapidly transformed the area of academic writing, offering benefits for idea generation, drafting feedback. Recent research shows that gen AI tools redistribute cognitive effort shifting focus away from information gathering and problem solving toward managing and evaluating AI generated content. However, over reliance and time pressure can reduce cognitive engagement, which is especially alarming in novice writers. Academic writing is not just about producing text. It's a reciprocal process of thinking and composing, involving cognitive processes, metacognitive strategies, and writing beliefs. As instructors, we're trying to achieve cognitive engagement in our students, a psychological state in which students put in a lot of effort to truly understand topic. So what I built to enable learning in my classroom is verify a systematic approach to AI literacy, structured around six components, verify sources and facts, evaluate logical flow, review rhetoric strategies, identify biases and assumptions, formulate feedback, and your contribution. I built it based on my own experience. Recent research and simplicity, trying to make it as simple as possible. Verifies based on constructivist learning theory. Students actively create meaning, not just receive it. Iterative process. Students may cycle through components multiple times in one assignment, recent research, and most importantly, AI specific challenges in academic writing that cannot be accounted for by traditional frameworks. Some of these challenges are hallucinations, coherence issues, lack of understanding on the side of predictive AI and rhetorical inconsistencies. The first component requires students to authenticate AI generated citations, factual claims and reference materials directly addressing hallucination effects. Some of the activities would require students to verify mixed AI generated in real citations to practice prompting to crosscheck factual claims using different AI tools, and come up with a chart like this generated with chat GPT. Finally, my students would develop personalized VER verification workflows that are tailored to their specific academic discipline, bearing in mind the fact that they come from different majors. The second component evaluates the logical flow of ideas, involves analyzing premise, conclusion, relationships, identifying logical inconsistencies, mapping conceptual connections between ideas. Students would analyze paragraphs and identify issues with flow, logical progression, argument structure. They would analyze AI generated essays, identifying logical leaps, repetitive patterns, disconnect between sections. One of the activities they liked the best was thesis to evidence mapping, where they had to extract the central thesis from AI generated text and map supporting evidence first individually. And then with the help of chat GPT, they would come up with a mind map that looks like this. One last activity in E component would require students to prompt different AI tools to generate coherence analysis and compare results. Our component stands for reviewing rhetorical strategies. Students had to check for audience awareness, genre conventions, various persuasive strategies and istic markers in AI generated content. Identifying biases and assumptions meant actually analyzing AI text for implicit cultural, political and disciplinary biases. Cross-referencing claims with multiple AI tools to reveal different perspectives and encourage questioning who benefits from this framing and what's missing. Feedback is a very important component of student writing. Traditional instructor feedback often remains unread. AI generated feedback is easily accessible, personalized, and shows amazing potential to support writing improvement. However, what I've noticed and what research has proven is that our students have deficiency in feedback literacy. We also need to keep in mind that AI generated feedback demonstrates limitations, hallucinations over praise, over inference, superficial analysis, poor actionability for complex revisions. In order to provide my students with a unified feedback response, I have created custom made GPT, which is argumentative, SA tutor. My students interacted with GPT and engaged in reiterative essay writing in which they would be searching for a perfect prompt thesis statement through a variety of feedback prompts, especially designed for coherence evidence structure. Once they came up with this custom made essay, they would be getting en engaged in peer feedback, and then they would compare their responses with AI feedback. They would also evaluate that AI generated feedback to, to find out some of the shortcomings that I already mentioned. The last, but the most important component is your contribution. Students are supposed to identify their own unique academic voice and perspective. They actually need to connect AI generated content with personal experience. Once they're done, they would write AI contribution statements for different sections, and in that way, I would, uh, encourage them to disclose AI assistance ethically. What impact did Verify have? This case study was conducted at Gulf University for Science and Technology, and it involved first year academic writing core students. It was applied in one section and there was a control group that received standard instruction. The verify framework improved students' AI literacy. Their results were better, their mental engagement strategies improved even positive gains. Small but positive were noticed in bias recognition and risk awareness. Qualitative analysis showed differences in critical thinking, metacognitive skills, verification practices, and academic integrity. Some of the student voices from Verify Group, um, were, are, are presented here, and as you can see, they're using numbers, uh, as a form of steps to show how they approach AI generated content. What I also noticed is that in the group with No Verify framework, students were slightly afraid of using ai, thinking that it may weaken their thinking skills. Where Verify was implemented, students were more self-confident and they had a more practical approach to it. Using it for help, but writing my own words, personal style, asking more questions like, why did you do this? And how come not just ask, give me the answer to sum up, verify, can be a good solution to AI generated writing analysis. What we need to keep in mind is that we cannot and should not ban AI in our classrooms. Let's see what happens if students use it thoughtfully, critically, and with purpose. Thank you.
